author: @maxerhard
text: I thought magnetostriction applied specifically to materials which change shape in a magnetic field, like a magnetic equivalent to piezoelectric materials. This is just straightforward magnetic attraction isn't it?

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text: zoom in to the Nano scale & look again, guaranteed this macro scale magnetostriction is also happening there, KISS

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it does - but it also applies to composite materials that do the same thing - this is a macro scale composite - so basically you can see what is happening - as I said in the video if we mixed wire with silicon we would get a smaller scale composite which is the topic of lots of research papers - I read a few lol

author: @paulmaydaynight9925
text:
so... moving the magnet gives a 'better' focused sound than moving the coil...
-perhaps a bit of [ink dipped/dried stiffened] carbon felt replacing the rubber will produce a 'better' base frequency response as it will travel a larger distance for the given input-
-lol ottoth 2 magnets one coil for the win, small hard rubber > small magnet for tweeter response + outer carbon felt ring> microwave magnet for the base-

author: @davidoneill2466
text:
the best part of your videos is watching how much fun you have making it…..

author: @lopsumtathro
text: just the coil will work, no iron core needed, i made a speaker this way a few years ago

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text:
sure, you can just wrap the bunch of magnet's in a cardboard tube & wrap/ -conductive paint- a fine coil on the outside of the tube then fix the tube ridged to the 'speaker' frame, but the core helps increase/enhance the changing flux magnetic field mass & give the moving solid magnet a fixed static centre to return to, be it sealed air gap/rubber/carbon felt or just a springed gap depending on what elasticity movement you require etc.
think Professor Eric Laithwaite's jumping ac magnetic aluminium rings ,the more you add the higher they rise

author: @talamacus73
text: as a mature student within the faculty of science and engineering at the University of Hull it is always fascinating to find out new discoveries made at your home base, love the content might be shocking the neighbours in time to come

replies.author: @AndreaDingbatt
replies.text:
My elder sister went to Hull Uni!!
(Many, MANY,  years ago!!)
I'd go down to visit her, and I used to wonder why the Bar staff were bleeping horrible to me and the Now Mr Dingbatt,,,, 
~apparently, because we were Punks,,,
~they thought we were Art Students!! Lol!😆
😆  Very Bad  form That!!

She was doing Dutch and Economics,at the time, I had No Idea Why...

Until,,, she got Married to a Multimillionaire ,,who happened to be from Holland.
He was a nice enough lad, I went to the wedding....

Sorry about the impromptu trip down memory lane for me, I've just had A Birthday...
Cake Too small with Wayyyy Too many Candles, set off the fire alarms...
Namaste 🙏
Have a great time at Uni!!
Andrea and Critters. ..XxX...

author: @Alacritous
text: That is so obvious, but it was the missing piece I've been looking for for a prank device. How to make a speaker that doesn't look like a speaker.  A device you can attach unobtrusively somewhere that will make sounds like children playing in the distance or a child laughing in a place where they shouldn't be. Something creepy like that.  Now I just have to make it.  Cheers!

author: @karlmyers6518
text: Could you use something like a balloon and Would that change the way it behaves .

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text: sure ,what is an inflated balloon but a contained collection of compressed fluid... one crevette being you want the input sound to be linear transferred to the other side for optimal transfer... think restricting the balloon sides in a rigid pipe or net as you inflate it, the higher the pressure ,the more gas in the given restricted space, try a modified bike tire in a pipe with a end magnet diaphragm on one end
